name: Study 2: Tactics Found at the 800 Level

comments.text:
For this second study on missed tactics, we'll see some missed opportunities in a game between two beginners. 

This was sent by a subscriber who played with the Black pieces, and both players are rated around 800 online. 

This game is a great example of how many tactics can occur in a chess game and how they can be missed!

Now the piece material was equal, but White was still up a pawn with a slight advantage.

Their knight was now under attack.

Going for ...f6 here attacks the knight on e5.

The point is to force them to back away from controlling the c6-square.

Now White had the correct idea in mind.

With the passed e- and h-pawns, Black still had the advantage, but this was a correct defensive setup.

Sure, Black gets the bishop on e2, but their king is on a silly square deep in White's position.

Black has two pieces for a rook here, but White is up two pawns and completely winning.
Black was about to get a queen.
Black continued developing as well, delaying capturing in the center for one more move.

The bishop activated to g5, immediately putting pressure on the knight.

If ...e6 is played at any moment, the knight becomes pinned.
White thought they could maybe promote if they traded rooks...
Black recaptured.

This is the last moment where White could have saved the game.

They needed to play Rh5, not to let Black's king in.

Instead, they gave a check on the sixth rank.
Black's king went up and attacked the knight, while also freeing up some space for the rook.

However, Black played the second best move and went for a queen trade.

It might seem like Black is not lost if White trades queens...
However, they activated the bishop to e2, which was too passive and gave Black the chance to get back into the game.

Black can jump back to c6 and put pressure on the rook on d8.

If the rook moves, however, the knight on e8 is hanging.

So, the best move for White here is to give up the exchange with Rc8, which is definitely unpleasant and gives up all the advantage.
However, they gave a check first.
White can grab the rook on f7.
Black ignored White's ideas and captured on f2, which was a losing mistake.

However, now White saw they could attack the rook in the corner.

It wasn't a fork anymore, though.
Black simply slid to f2, and now there were two dangerous pawns about to promote.
White retreated to e3 with check...
Once Black moved...

There was an amazing tactic here that both sides missed.

But, to be fair, it's really hard to see the forced line.

The idea is to capture on f7 with check and give up the knight.
They did make a mistake, however, counterattacking the bishop on g3.
White retreated to the center with check...

White missed the immediate fork and captured on f6.

This is still winning...
It turned out that White wanted to remove the defender of the d7-square and fork the two rooks!
If Black captures...
Black started pushing the other passed pawn they had...

And a great response by White.

Instead of retreating, they placed the queen on a4 from where it pinned the knight.
The simple fork on c7 wins here for White.
But they missed that in this line White can escape by giving a check on d6.

Which continued moving forward, this time to the center.

Did this move run right into a tactic that Black missed?

But they really liked giving checks.

The computer approves of this way of playing, but only if you have a concrete plan in mind.

From the next moves you'll see that White didn't and probably just wanted to check Black.

Playing without a plan is not good and can often get you tangled up, as you'll see.

And White won back the pawn, putting more pressure on the pinned knight on f6.

The queen was also extremely vulnerable on the d-file.

Black's position was falling apart so much that the computer suggests ...Qxd5 for Black here, giving up the queen!
And now Black's king was simply too active and about to escort the e-pawn.
But couldn't save both of them.
Once Black reached h4, it might have been smart for White to place the rook on the h7-square, behind the pawn.

However, White castled long first, which was also a good move.

Now e4 is an idea again, as the d5-pawn is pinned.

Black slid the bishop to g3 and attacked both the knight and the pawn on h4.

White can't save both in this position...
First, White gave a check to the king.

Now Rd6+ is extremely strong, as there is no pawn on the f-file to block.

The king has to run.
If White just retreats to f3, for example...
Black was able to save one of the pawns...

Black continued developing and opened up the dark-squared bishop, although pinning their knight on f6.

Here, a move like e4 is great, challenging Black's center and trying to place a knight on e4.

Black kept pushing, as they should have.

It was up to White to decide how they could save their position.
But this only helped Black get even deeper into the position, with tempo again.
Black replied with ...d5, placing a pawn in the center.
But they decided to give up the exchange here, with another concrete idea in mind!
But the d8-square could be covered by the bishop.
White opened with the Reti, a flexible first move.
After that, White has h4 check, forcing the king to the f-file, which is the whole idea of the sacrifice.
White went for their idea.
Which gave White an opportunity to take control of the e3-square.
But only if White goes for the fork now.

Black played a very interesting idea here and moved forward, putting pressure on the knight, although it was defended.

In many endgames, the king joining the party early can be the game changer.
White tried somehow getting the knight into the game, but they were too late.
Yes, White captured the bishop on c7...
Still, they played the best move, reactivating the knight on d3.

White correctly captured in the center.

The reasoning is that since White's rook and Black's queen were on the same file, opening up the center helped White, while also allowing them to coordinate better.

Going for e5 wouldn't be as potent because of ...h6.
Black escaped with one of the rooks...

Black correctly defended with ...Rf8.

Now it was important for White to unpin their knight with Rd6+.
They still played the best move here, attacking the pawn on f7.
Another desperate check by White.
Now Black played ...Rc7, aiming to reactivate the rook on the c-file and put pressure on the c2-pawn.

But there was a queen on the board.

Next, Black would either win the knight or the pawn on d7 or both by giving checks and forking White.

White resigned here.

What a great example of a tactical game that could have gone either way had the sides realized their tactics in time!

And Black correctly retreated to f8.

Being up a pawn, one of the winning plans for White here is to simply continue developing, with something like Rd2, b4 and getting the light-squared bishop out.

White wanted to solidify the knight here, but there was no need to.

A move like h4+ instead was better, forcing the king away.
Black slid the rook to f2 now, maybe with the idea of getting it to f3 to help the pawn or to f7 to try and trade rooks.

White didn't want to take and developed the queenside knight instead.

Maybe leaving room open for the c-pawn to move and creating more tension with c4 was better, but that's not the topic of this study.

White tried playing Rh5 now.

The idea was to get the rook to the d5-square and check Black's king from d5, in order to get to d7.
Black continued developing with ...Bc5 and attacked the pawn on f2.
There is a skewer on the f-file.

The best move for Black here was probably something like ...Ne4, attacking the bishop and the knight on c3.

However, Black took in the center.

Black slid to h6 here, escaping with the king.

Now White's knight on e8 was pinned to the rook.

Black decided to capture on h4, and now the material was equal.

The computer claims that this position is in the balance.

However I would argue that from a human standpoint, Black has all the advantage now with more active pieces and the advanced king.

White suddenly had a fork on c5!

This move attacked the king and two pawns, on e6 and on b7.

White simply takes the rook and is winning because of the material advantage and more active pieces.

Also, it's important to note that White will be able to save the knight on the next move, as Black can't trap it in any way.
But Black saw the way to victory and realized that the trade is completely fine.

And Black went for the aforementioned trade.

Trading here would be fatal for White, as there would be no way to stop both of Black's pawns.

Keep in mind that the c7-square was covered by Black's bishop.

Black missed the best move and attacked the rook on d8.

Their idea was to remove the defender of the e8-knight.

Black simply escaped to g7.

Trading rooks here keeps White with a much better position...

They probably missed that the rook from g2 defends the bishop.

And this is where White's downfall started.

White correctly avoided the trade here and gave a check.

Trading on h4 would leave them in a knight vs. bishop endgame which is almost always unfavorable for the side that has the knight.

Also, White has to be wary of Black's active king.

This simply allowed Black's king to get into the position, and there was no defending anymore.

White's passed pawn could never really promote, and with the help of their king, Black's definitely could.

Black recaptured, and there was a tactic that won for White.

However, they missed it.

They went for the g2-weakness instead.

Here, White was supposed to give a check on the d-file and then push the c-pawn.

But that is hard to see.
White had to keep pushing their passed pawn.
Black could have just pushed the e-pawn here, but they decided to bring the king even closer before doing that.

Well, White decided to trade on c4, and it turns out the trade isn't really helpful for their position.

They are trading a bishop for a knight and letting Black activate their rook on c4.

Also, they are leaving their knight on g4 under attack.

They saved the knight by jumping back to f2.

Maybe a move like Nd3 is the idea.

However, there was a small fork that they missed.
Now they reactivated the rook on the seventh rank, attacking the h-pawn...

Black played the best move here, attacking the rook and the knight behind it.

White could have saved both pieces with Neg4+ here...

Black recaptured on c4.

Now both their bishop and their rook were on active squares, while White had to make some moves to untangle their own pieces.

But Black's position was perfectly set up for the pawn to push forward.

The h4- and the h2-squares were covered by the bishop.
White decided to retreat to f6 instead and save the knight.
Wherever the king goes...
The only way for White to somehow try and get back into the game is to bring the knight back.
Black should have blocked with the pawn here, but they decided to block with the bishop, which allowed for a similar tactic that both sides missed.
Which helped White's queen activate to the d4-square.
Once Black's king moves...

But the e-pawn was one step closer to promotion.

Black could have also pushed the h-pawn here, as the rook had abandoned the h-file.
Which they did.

White immediately gets the piece back and saves the knight on e8.

To be fair, this tactic was easier to see.
If Black captures it...

However, they didn't.

They recaptured on f6 with check, winning a pawn.

They were still much better, though.

Black went for the immediate ...c5, gambiting a pawn.

This variation is unusual but can be played.
Once Black moves...
It allowed Black to get the knight back onto the more active c4-square, supported by the rook.
But not the other.

And this was White's plan.

They were persistent to attack Black's king.

Maybe they thought they could somehow orchestrate a checkmating attack.

And White continued with d4, grabbing some central space themselves.

We'd transposed into the Queen's Pawn opening.

Black moved to the most natural f5-square.

If you were to choose, would you reckon that the trade on c4 is good for White?
They jumped back to g4 with check.
Finally, White realized that the knight on g4 was defended and moved the pawn up to h4, with check.

So, we've reached the endgame.

As in the other game phases, piece activity is one of, if not the most important aspect.

Here, Black improved the position of their bishop with check, although a move like ...Nc6, getting the knight back into the game, was better.
White slid to b1, naturally.

White could have captured on f7 here once again.

The idea is to remove the defender of the bishop on e6.
White attacked the bishop and covered the promotion square, but they were too late.
The only move is ...Kg5.

However, White ignored the threat and played the best move!

An active and strong center was more valuable than the pawn on f2.

White made some space for their king not to get checkmated on the back rank.

However, this move was pretty unnecessary and passive.
A good move by Black here, developing with tempo...
Naturally, Black captured.
The rook escaped to b8.

Maybe Black should have gone forward here.

Instead, they probably wanted to hang around White's pawns.
But they weren't the only one with passed pawns!
White recaptured, and they were one square away from promotion...
